source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.3.3-13.3.7 network tools (pp. 240-243)

questions.q: Which two older commands does mtr replace?
questions.why: mtr is a network diagnostic tool that replaces ping and traceroute and is faster.

questions.q: Which older command does ss replace, and what is it used for?
options: netstat - to view the status of ports and sockets
arp - to view the ARP table
questions.why: ss replaces the old netstat and shows socket/port status.

questions.q: View the local machine's listening TCP and UDP ports with numeric addresses and the owning processes.
questions.why: -t TCP, -u UDP, -l listening, -n numeric, -p process: the classic 'what's listening' one-liner.

questions.q: In ss, what do the -l and -p options do?
options: -l shows only listening sockets; -p shows the processes using the sockets
-l shows the local port only; -p shows the peer
-l is for TCP; -p is for UDP
questions.why: -l filters to listening sockets and -p adds the owning process info (-a all, -n numeric, -t TCP, -u UDP).

questions.q: Show the properties (speed, duplex, link) of the NIC ens160.
questions.why: ethtool <device> reports NIC properties such as Speed, Duplex, Port and Link detected.

questions.q: Show the broadcast address of 192.168.100.20/24 using ipcalc.
questions.why: ipcalc -b prints the broadcast; -n network, -m netmask, -p prefix, -h hostname via DNS.

questions.q: Query this machine's public (WAN) IPv4 address using curl and ifconfig.me.
questions.why: curl -4 ifconfig.me returns your public IPv4; curl -6 ifconfig.me returns the IPv6.
